data.title: Femasys announces pricing of underwritten public offering and concurrent private placement with gross proceeds of $4.5M
data.description:
• Femasys (NASDAQ:FEMY) on Friday announced the pricing of an underwritten public offering of 3.6M shares of its common stock at a public offering price of $0.85 per share and a separate concurrent private placement of 1.68M shares of its common stock at a price of $0.85 per share sold to certain existing institutional stockholders of Femasys and a price of $1.02 per share to certain of its directors and officers.
  • The gross proceeds of the public offering and private placement to Femasys are expected to be about $4.5 million.
  • Femasys intends to use the net proceeds from the public offering and the private placement for expansion of commercial efforts, development of its products and product candidates, general corporate purposes, capital expenditures, working capital and general and administrative expenses.
  • FEMY is -13.72% to $0.88.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Synopsys suspends sales in China to comply with US export curbs - report
data.description:
Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) has directed its employees in China to suspend services and sales in the country and stop taking new orders to adhere to new U.S. export curbs, Reuters reported citing an internal letter.

Products impacted include design software and chemicals for semiconductors, the report added.

Synopsys declined to comment following a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

On Thursday, only a day after Synopsys, released its second quarter results and guidance, the company suspended its guidance for the quarter in progress and full-year fiscal 2025 due to new export controls related to China. Synopsys said it received a letter from the Bureau of Industry and Security of the U.S. Department of Commerce informing the company of new export restrictions related to China.

Synopsys had said then that it was assessing the potential impact of the BIS Letter on its business, operating results and financial condition.

On Wednesday it was reported that the BIS was sending letters to electronic design automation, or EDA, companies directing them to stop selling hardware, software and services to China, in an effort to slow China's development of advanced semiconductors.

The internal letter sent to employees in China said, "based on our initial interpretation, these new restrictions broadly prohibit the sales of our products and services in China and are effective as of May 29, 2025," as per the report.

To comply, Synopsys said it was stopping sales and fulfillment in China and halting new orders until it gets further clarification, the report noted.

The actions affect all customers in China, including employees of global customers working at sites in China and Chinese military users wherever they are located, according to the letter.

In addition, the letter noted that the Chinese customers' access to the company's customer support portal SolvNetPlus has been disabled.

Other companies potentially affected by the new controls include Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), Keysight Technologies (KEYS) and Ansys (ANSS), which is currently in the process of being acquired by Synopsys.

Synopsys, Cadence and Siemens's Mentor Graphics control over 70% of China's EDA market, Chinese state news agency Xinhua reported in April.

Shares of Synopsys were largely flat on Friday.

Update: The story was updated with Synopsys declining to comment, and stock price movement.

data.title: Newsmax stockholders to sell 121M shares
data.description:
• Shares of Newsmax (NYSE:NMAX) are down by double-digits in heavy trading after the company said certain shareholders intend to sell more than 121M shares.
  • The 121M shares consist of 81M shares of class B common stock issued to shareholders; 600K shares issued to shareholders upon the exercise of certain warrants; and ~40M shares issuable to an affiliate of CEO Christopher Ruddy.
  • The company will not receive any proceeds from these sales.
  • NMAX is down 18%. 
  • Source: SEC filing

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow pare some losses as inflation gauge comes in-line
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages pared some losses on Friday as the inflation gauge came in-line, while investors awaited any more news related to President Trump's tariffs.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.1%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.1%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped 1 basis point to 4.41%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.94%.

The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit on Thursday issued an administrative stay blocking a ruling from a lower court on Wednesday that struck down “reciprocal” tariffs Donald Trump imposed in April.

So even if the court ruling is upheld, it doesn’t look like the world is heading back to the pre-2025 tariff situation, even if there is a pathway to a world with less tariffs after the ruling, Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

The Fed's preferred inflation gauge rose 0.1% in April, as expected.

Wall Street's major averages on Thursday ended with marginal gains amid a bond rally sparked by weak GDP data and more back-and-forth on tariffs.

“Risk assets initially surged yesterday after the surprise court ruling, but they gave up those gains later in the day as markets digested several alternative avenues they could pursue,” Reid said.

The U.S. trade deficit narrowed in April.

Furthermore, Chicago PMI data slides further into contraction territory in May as data came in at 40.5 versus the forecasted 45.0 level.

data.title: Meyer Burger closes Arizona solar panel plant in latest blow to industry in U.S.
data.description:
Swiss solar manufacturer Meyer Burger (OTCPK:MYBUF) said Thursday it has shut down module production activities at its manufacturing facility in Goodyear, Arizona, due to financial troubles, laying off all 283 employees.

The layoffs were disclosed in a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification, or WARN letter, filed with the Arizona Department of Economic Security on May 22, as the company has been "unable to obtain the refinancing that would enable it to continue the affected operations."

The closure is a setback for the solar industry in the U.S. to build a domestic supply chain and reduce its reliance on China, and adds to Meyer Burger's (OTCPK:MYBUF) troubles, whose operations in Europe and the U.S. have struggled to compete with cheaper products imported from Asia.

The Arizona plant, built to capitalize on clean energy incentives introduced during the Biden administration, closed after just one year, and the company canceled plans for a second U.S. factory in Colorado last year.

data.title: Yatra signals 20% gross margin and 30% EBITDA growth for FY26 as corporate and MICE segments accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Yatra Online, Inc. (YTRA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Dhruv Shringi highlighted strong annual and quarterly results, noting "For FY’25, we are pleased to report annual revenues of INR7.9 billion, which is approximately USD93.1 million, up 90% year-over-year." Shringi emphasized that "our full year revenue reflects the momentum we have built across our corporate travel and MICE businesses, we have been pivotal in navigating a competitive landscape."
  • Shringi detailed the continued expansion in the corporate segment, noting "In Q4, we added 35 new corporate clients contributing to INR1.4 billion in expected annual volumes. For fiscal year 2025, we secured 148 new clients contributing to INR7.5 billion in expected annual volumes."
  • He reported that Yatra's MICE business is "a standout performer with significant growth and margin expansion in the fourth quarter" and said that the company expects to become "one of the top three players in this fast-run segment in the current fiscal year itself."
  • Shringi introduced a new technology milestone, stating, "Yatra became one of the first travel management companies in India to integrate the new distribution capabilities that the airlines are offering," which he described as "a transformative technology developed in conjunction with IATA, by the airlines."
  • Shringi also addressed B2C Air, stating "In Q4, our gross bookings sell only 6%. The silver lining is that we've seen stabilization in our B2C Air business now despite facing competitive headwinds."
  • The CEO described advances in AI, highlighting the rollout of "our AI-enabled low fare finding, a smart post-booking fare optimization tool" and the use of bots to "significantly drive down the cost of servicing in the near-term."
  • On share convertibility, Shringi stated, "We have defined a structure that we believe works and allows for this convertibility. While there are still some hurdles to overcome, we are confident in the structures viability."
  • For FY '26, Shringi gave guidance: "we're introducing preliminary guidance of 20% growth in revenue less service cost or gross margin, and 30% adjusted EBITDA growth driven by three pillars: expansion in corporate travel; continued scaling of MICE and hotels; and full cost synergies from Globe Travel."
  • CFO Anuj Kumar Sethi reported, "For the fourth quarter of financial year 2025, revenue from operations grew 114% year-on-year to INR2,192 million, approximately USD25.7 million driven by a 54% increase in hotels and packages growth in MICE and inorganic growth contributions."
  • Sethi added, "Adjusted EBITDA was up 28% year-on-year to INR90 million approximately USD1.1 million, yielding an 8.2% adjusted EBITDA to gross margin."

Outlook

  • Management introduced preliminary guidance for FY '26, targeting "20% growth in revenue less service cost or gross margin, and 30% adjusted EBITDA growth."
  • The company expects continued momentum in corporate client acquisition, MICE segment growth, and technology investment as drivers of future growth.
  • Shringi noted, "we are confident of achieving the guidance that we are setting ourselves."

Financial Results

  • Yatra reported revenue from operations for Q4 FY25 of INR2,192 million (USD25.7 million), citing a 114% year-over-year increase.
  • Gross margins (revenue less service costs) for the quarter were INR1,094 million (USD12.8 million), up 34% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was INR90 million (USD1.1 million), up 23% year-over-year, and 8.2% as a percentage of gross margin.
  • For the full year FY25, revenue from operations reached INR7,957 million (USD93.1 million), with adjusted EBITDA of INR344 million (USD4 million), and net profit turning positive at INR24 million (USD0.3 million).
  • Cash and equivalents plus term deposits as of March 31, 2025, stood at INR1,960 million (USD23 million), while gross debt decreased to INR546 million (USD6.4 million).

Q&A

  • Scott Buck, H.C. Wainwright: "If the situation were to further deteriorate along the Pakistan border, how much of the business has historically been tied to that region?" CEO Shringi: "the northern part of India accounts for almost about 25% to 30% of our overall business volumes. ...I think it's safe to say that 30-plus percent of the business would get impacted if something was to escalate a bit further."
  • Buck: "what can you tell us about the proposed corporate structure? And what does that mean for share fungibility?" Shringi: "we've got, I think a structure in place that now we think, works... Now is the process of just going through and making sure that all the procedures are associated with that structure gets put in place over the course of the next couple of months."
  • Buck: Regarding MICE and acquisitions: "Are there acquisition opportunities out there...?" Shringi: "we continue to evaluate that opportunities. We've done one about six months back...I think that should get done in the current quarter itself, and that will then free us up to start looking at other opportunities going forward."
  • Buck: "Can you grow the business 50% from here without having to add meaningfully on the cost side?" Shringi: "We think we can at least grow to 30% to -- 40% without needing to change the cost structure significantly. That much growth, we should be able to achieve in the numbers that you see, the escalation in operating cost...is largely due to legal and professional fees...Otherwise, our cost structure has changed year-over-year only because of the addition of Globe's numbers."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral, with questions focused on geopolitical risk exposure, structural changes, growth capacity, and MICE acquisition strategy. There was no evident skepticism or pressing of management beyond seeking clarity on near-term risks and growth levers.
  • Management's tone remained confident and forward-looking during both prepared remarks and Q&A. Phrases such as "we are confident of achieving the guidance that we are setting ourselves" and "we are dedicating all our efforts to making this a reality" indicate strong confidence levels.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared slightly more assertive regarding guidance and integration milestones, while the analyst tone maintained a steady, fact-seeking approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY26 was explicitly introduced this quarter, targeting 20% growth in gross margin and 30% adjusted EBITDA, while last quarter focused on expectations of sustained margin expansion without specific figures.
  • The MICE business remains a central theme, but this call included more detail on integration progress and projected market leadership within the current fiscal year.
  • The previous quarter emphasized initial progress on corporate restructuring; the current quarter indicates that a viable structure is now defined, with implementation underway.
  • Analysts continued to focus on corporate structure, MICE growth, and scalability, but geopolitical risk was a new focus this quarter due to recent regional tensions.
  • Management’s confidence increased, as reflected in more definitive language regarding guidance and operational progress.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified geopolitical instability in northern India as a risk, noting "April began on a strong note for us. But following the unfortunate incident in Pahalgam...and the subsequent escalation of tensions between India and Pakistan, we experienced a temporary disruption in travel activity."
  • Shringi quantified the risk, stating up to "30-plus percent of the business would get impacted if something was to escalate a bit further."
  • Management reported proactive mitigation, including "working proactively with our airline and hotel partners to support customers through flexible cancellation and rebooking options."
  • The process of structural conversion to India shares remains complex and ongoing, with management unable to specify a timeline.

Final Takeaway

Yatra’s Q4 FY25 call demonstrated robust top-line and margin growth, fueled by aggressive expansion in corporate travel and the MICE segment, as well as successful integration of recent acquisitions. Management set forth ambitious FY26 guidance of 20% gross margin and 30% EBITDA growth, underpinned by operational discipline, technological innovation, and a scalable cost structure. The company remains vigilant to geopolitical risks but expressed confidence in its mitigation strategies and continued upward trajectory. The ongoing corporate restructuring process and potential for additional MICE acquisitions present further upside, positioning Yatra for continued leadership in India’s rapidly expanding travel market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gap's tariff projection, uneven performance continues to drag down shares
data.description:
Gap’s (NYSE:GAP) uneven results for the first quarter  and the company’s worse-case scenario regarding tariffs continues to weigh on shares Friday’s, with the stock expected to spend a fifth straight day in the red.

After Thursday’s close, Gap’s (NYSE:GAP) sales-driven EPS beat was overshadowed by FY25 guidance which did not incorporate a tariff headwind of as much as $300M (without mitigation efforts). This outsized outlook, coupled with inconsistent performances by its Banana Republic and Athleta banners, led Wall Street analysts to lower EPS and gross margin estimates for the retailer.

Gap’s (NYSE:GAP) tariff-impacted guidance implies a ~10% cut to its prior EBIT/EPS outlook, Morgan Stanley’s Alex Straton says, adding that “this stands out to the downside compared to select peers, who pointed to a more moderate or fully mitigated tariff headwinds.”

The good news, Straton says, is this peer outperformance suggests potential upside to Gap’s tariff-impacted guidance.

“We see no reason GAP should not be able to mitigate tariffs to the same extent [as peers] especially given its greater scale and China sourcing exposure.”

But the post-earnings sell-off can also be attributed to the inconsistent performances of certain banners, overly bullish positioning prior to the results, and softer second quarter guidance.

While comparable sales at Gap and Old Navy were positives, the turnaround at Athleta is expected to take more time, and Banana Republic could continue to weigh on top-line growth and prevent a full sales recovery, UBS’s Jay Sole says.

Gap CEO Richard Dickson also acknowledged the challenges for Athleta and Banana Republic.

“Our outlook assumes strength at Old Navy and Gap, stabilizing performance at Banana Republic, and a longer recovery time at Athleta,” he said on an earnings call with analysts.

Unfortunately, these measures will require additional investment at a time when all levers to offset tariffs need to be employed.

Morgan Stanley’s Straton warns that there is already a $150M cost reduction plan in place. Additional investments could be cut, but that could impede the long-term health of the company, something management seems obviously committed to.

“We see GAP at an inflection point to support low-to-mid single digit sales growth, annual operating margin expansion targeting historical levels of profitability,” J.P. Morgan’s Matthew Boss says, maintaining an Overweight rating on the stock.

Despite the outsized reaction to the company's latest quarter, Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors remain bullish on Gap, rating the stock as a Buy. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating is also confident in Gap’s (GAP) outlook, with a Strong Buy rating and Quant score of 4.52 out of 5.

data.title: Fmr. Kansas City Fed President: ‘I’d be surprised if inflation moves down much at all’
data.description:
Former Kansas City Fed President Esther George said the Federal Reserve is not behind schedule on rate cuts despite political pressure, as inflation remains above target though not accelerating.

In an interview with CNBC, George defended the Federal Open Market Committee’s data-focused approach amid recent discussions about the timing of potential rate reductions.

“Today’s inflation report, for example, tells us that inflation is not accelerating. That is good news,” George stated. “It doesn’t, however, tell us that we are making further progress toward the Fed’s two percent inflation target.”

Her comments come following a meeting between Fed Chair Jerome Powell and President Donald Trump, who has publicly advocated for faster rate cuts.

George expressed skepticism about significant inflation decreases in the near term.

“I’d be surprised if inflation’s going to move down much at all. That would be my own forecast,” she said, pointing to the nation’s fiscal situation as a primary concern rather than focusing exclusively on tariff impacts.

She emphasized that ongoing downward pressure on inflation will likely come mainly from the Fed maintaining its current policy stance.

In addition, while oil price (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) declines offer some relief in headline inflation numbers, George highlighted persistent housing costs as an ongoing inflationary pressure. She also noted that workforce dynamics, including federal worker departures and shifting immigration policies, could impact future inflation trajectories in unpredictable ways.

“I think the story has not yet been written on how this ends up,” he concluded.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: CGC, MRVL, PATH, GAP, and more
data.description:
Stock futures dipped Friday after the latest PCE inflation data aligned with expectations, while renewed trade tensions resurfaced following President Donald Trump's claim that China breached its preliminary trade deal, raising concerns over a potential prolonged trade conflict.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Ulta Beauty (NASDAQ:ULTA) +8% – The stock climbed after the beauty retailer delivered upbeat Q1 results and lifted its full-year outlook, citing a strong start to FY2025. Ulta now projects FY2025 net sales between $11.5B and $11.7B, slightly widening its prior range of $11.5B–$11.6B and bracketing the Street's $11.59B estimate. Adjusted EPS is now forecast at $22.65 to $23.20, up from the earlier $22.50 to $22.90, and surrounding the $22.96 consensus. Comparable sales are expected to range from flat to +1.5%, compared to the earlier projection of flat to +1.0%.
  • UiPath (NYSE:PATH) +11% – Shares rallied after the company posted strong Q1 results and issued upbeat guidance for Q2 and the full year. For Q2, UiPath expects revenue between $345M and $350M, comfortably above the Street’s estimate of $331.82M. Annualized recurring revenue (ARR) is forecast in the range of $1.715B to $1.720B by the end of July, with non-GAAP operating income of around $40M. For the FY2026, revenue is projected at $1.549B to $1.554B, above the $1.52B consensus, with ARR targeted at $1.820B to $1.825B and non-GAAP operating income of roughly $305M.
  • Zscaler (NASDAQ:ZS) +4% – Shares gained after the cybersecurity firm posted strong FQ3 results, highlighted by a 23% Y/Y increase in revenue. Calculated billings rose 25%, and deferred revenue climbed 26%. The company also named Kevin Rubin as its new CFO. Looking ahead, Zscaler guided FQ4 revenue between $705M and $707M, just below the $707.96M consensus, and expects non-GAAP EPS of $0.79–$0.80, above $0.77 consensus. For FY2025, it forecast revenue of $2.659B–$2.661B vs. the consensus of $2.65B, billings of $3.184B–$3.189B, and EPS of $3.18–$3.19, surpassing the consensus of $3.08.

Biggest stock losers

  • The Gap (NYSE:GAP) -15% – Shares tumbled as investors reacted to the company’s FY2025 guidance, which notably excluded the potential impact of proposed tariffs. Management warned that tariffs could hit operating income by $100M–$150M even after mitigation efforts. Meanwhile, Q2 net sales are expected to remain flat Y/Y at $3.72B, slightly below the $3.73B consensus. For the full year, Gap forecasts 1–2% sales growth, or $15.25B–$15.40B, roughly in line with estimates, and operating income of $1.19B–$1.21B, up 8–10% Y/Y.
  • American Eagle Outfitters (NYSE:AEO) -7% – Shares declined after a mixed Q1 report. Total revenue fell 5% Y/Y to $1.1B, with comparable sales down 3%. The Aerie brand posted a 4% drop in comps, while American Eagle brand comps slipped 2%. Gross margin contracted sharply to 29.6% from 40.6% a year earlier. For Q2, the company expects a 5% decline in revenue and a 3% drop in comparable sales, with projected operating income of $40M–$45M.
  • Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) -3% – Shares dipped despite the chipmaker posting Q1 results and forward guidance that exceeded analyst forecasts. For Q2, the company expects revenue of approximately $2B, plus or minus 5%, slightly above the $1.98B consensus. Adjusted EPS is projected at $0.67 (±$0.05), also ahead of the $0.66 analysts were expecting, suggesting continued strength in its end markets.
  • Canopy Growth (NASDAQ:CGC) -12% – Shares sank after the company posted weaker-than-expected FQ4 results, dragged down by underperformance in international markets and its Storz & Bickel segment. Adjusted gross margin fell 200 bps Y/Y to ~19%, leading to an adjusted EBITDA loss of C$9M and a GAAP loss per share of C$1.43, both missing Street forecasts. While quarterly free cash outflow worsened by ~60% Y/Y to C$36M, the full-year figure improved by ~24% to C$177M, aided by lower interest payments as total debt declined to C$304M from C$597M a year earlier. Management outlined plans to achieve at least C$20M in annualized cost savings over the next 12–18 months through SG&A and cost of sales efficiencies.

data.title: Consumer sentiment brightens up in May revision as inflation expectations moderate
data.description:
May University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: 52.2 vs. prior estimate of 50.8 and 52.2 in April.

Year-ahead inflation expectations: +6.6% vs. previous estimate of +7.3% and +6.5% in April

Five-year implied inflation:+4.2% vs. initial May estimate of +4.6% and +4.4% in April.

Current Conditions Index: 58.9. vs. 57.6 prior estimate and 59.8 in April.

Expectations Index: 47.9 vs. 46.5 prior estimate and 47.3 in the previous month.
